project_overview.project_name: Public Financial Management and Institutional Strengthening Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve and build capacity for budget preparation and implementation
project_overview.beneficiaries: Ministry of Finance and Planning, line ministries (Agriculture, Education, Health, Gender/Child/Social Welfare), National Audit Chamber, National Revenue Authority, Public Procurement and Disposal of Assets Authority, civil servants, citizens of South Sudan

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on central government and four priority sectors
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - entire country is fragile and conflict-affected. South Sudan experienced large-scale civil war in 2013 and 2016, with ongoing subnational conflict, weak institutions, and humanitarian crisis. Country is at high risk of debt distress and faces macroeconomic volatility

key_components.description: Support establishment of budget preparation processes with adequate arrangements, tools, and timely resource allocation. Includes strengthening revenue forecasting, developing budget calendar, harmonizing budget procedures, implementing supplementary budget reallocation procedures, managing arrears payment, and introducing gender considerations in budget planning. Implementation in focus sectors (agriculture, education, health, gender/social welfare).

key_components.component_name: Strengthening the Transparency and Predictability of Budget Implementation and Procurement for Priority Spending Areas
key_components.description: Streamline budget implementation processes and upgrade systems. Includes cash management strengthening, payroll verification and electronic payroll system implementation, FMIS upgrade to cloud-based services, oversight of subnational fund transfers through State Transfer Monitoring Committees, and public procurement system strengthening including PPDAA support, training, and e-government procurement development.

key_components.component_name: Supporting Institutions, Capacity Development and Facilitating Change for Public Financial Management Reform
key_components.description: Strengthen institutional and human capacity for PFM reform. Includes organizational review of MOFP, skills development in PFM functions with focus on women, establishment of professional accountancy organization, internal audit strengthening, National Audit Chamber capacity building, stakeholder engagement, budget literacy workshops, community monitoring groups, web-based budget information platform, and media partnerships.

Support project management, coordination, and monitoring and evaluation. Includes establishment of Project Management Unit in MOFP, recruitment of PMU staff, project monitoring using geo-enabled monitoring system (GEMS), grievance redress mechanism, and PMU training and operating costs. Fiduciary specialists shared with ECRP II.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance and Planning (MOFP), Republic of South Sudan
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Steering Committee (NSC) chaired by MOFP, meeting every six months, comprising undersecretaries from key ministries and agencies. National Technical Working Group (NTWG) chaired by MOFP, meeting quarterly, comprising MOFP technical staff and line ministry representatives. Project Management Unit (PMU) embedded in MOFP's Aid Coordination Unit, headed by government Program Director. Coordination with development partners through monthly donor PFM group and development partner PFM Technical Working Group. Alignment with PFM Oversight Committee and PFM Reform Strategy roadmap.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations (July 1, 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2020). Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) to be finalized during implementation. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents for goods, works, and non-consulting services. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system for planning and tracking. Prior and post-procurement reviews by World Bank. Procurement committee of 5-7 members appointed by MOFP Undersecretary. Senior procurement specialist in PMU provides coordination and capacity building. Direct payments preferred for consultants, contractors, and suppliers.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results framework with PDO and intermediate results indicators. Geo-Enabling Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) for real-time data collection using open-source tools, GPS coordinates, and automated geo-mapping. Semi-annual progress reporting. M&E specialist in PMU coordinates data collection with MOFP units and MDAs. Baselines and targets established for all indicators. Data sources include budget submissions, financial statements, payroll reports, procurement reviews, audit reports, and attendance records.

identified_risks.risk_description: Frequent changes in political leadership and appointees may undermine government ownership of project and slow reform gains. Complex political economy implications from resource injection and power dynamics reconfiguration in volatile environment.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Engage with multiple levels of leadership within targeted ministries to ensure broad institutional ownership. Link PFM reforms to Revitalized Agreement on Resolution of Conflict (R-ARCSS). Demonstrate early gains to appeal to government willingness for change. Ongoing review of key PFM legislation expected to provide strong basis for reforms.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risks related to successful implementation of policy adjustment, limited access to concessional financing, oil price volatility, and budget process weaknesses. Exchange rate and inflation volatility despite recent stabilization. High debt distress risk (59.5% of GDP). Dwindling oil production and non-diversified economy. Recent devastating floods (2021) with estimated damages of US$671 million.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IMF Staff Monitored Program (SMP) provides credible monitoring and review process with focus on fiscal discipline, monetary/exchange rate reform, debt management, and governance strengthening. Recent IMF SDR allocation (US$335 million) provides additional resources. Project will consider contingency arrangements to offset potential price increases. Coordination with IMF on reform priorities.

identified_risks.risk_description: Project incorporates new technologies and processes novel in South Sudan context. Complexity and novelty of technological solutions and processes may create implementation risks. Need for robust back-end IT infrastructure and management processes.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Anchor project in realistic sequence of activities within PFMRS Concept Note and Roadmap framework. Ensure necessary back-end IT infrastructure upgrades and management processes are robust before implementation. Close coordination with World Bank sectoral operations in focus sectors. Change facilitation component (3.2) to support measured and sustainable introduction of technological and institutional reforms.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Targeted institutions staffed with unskilled, aging, and/or absentee employees. Low staff morale due to poor compensation and slow salary payments. General lack of compliance with policies, standards, and procedures. Limited absorptive capacity for staff time in large-scale reforms. Sustainability risks due to capacity and resource constraints in scarce resource environment.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Multi-pronged approach involving partnerships with key stakeholders to produce qualified staff. Technical assistance and capacity building for key implementation aspects. Shared PMU managing both PFM project and ECRP II to facilitate fiduciary and administrative tasks. Proposed Multi-Donor Transition Trust Fund (MDTTF) to augment IDA resources and enhance implementation support. Periodic regular implementation support missions. Capacity building through on-the-job training, professional certification, twinning arrangements with regional institutions, and establishment of professional bodies.

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak public procurement oversight and challenges in budget preparation, implementation, and oversight. Inadequate accounting system, gaps in internal audit oversight, lack of clear approval and authorization arrangements, inadequate segregation of functions in payment processing. Risk of unsupported payments, delayed funds flow, delayed financial reporting. Soft expenditures (training, workshops) susceptible to abuse. Weak internal controls and oversight.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: PMU staffed with consultants responsible for fiduciary accountability and oversight. Financial Management specialist hired to strengthen oversight while building government capacity. Ring-fenced project funds through segregated Designated Account and cashbooks operated by PMU. Regular in-year internal audits by Internal Audit Directorate. Annual external audits by National Audit Chamber with technical support. Direct payment method for consultants and suppliers. Monthly bank reconciliation statements. Project FM manual detailing internal control arrangements. Capacity building initiatives for government staff on core PFM areas. Use of computerized accounting system for transaction recording and financial reporting.

identified_risks.risk_description: Incomplete public procurement framework with delayed operationalization of 2018 Act. Lack of functional procurement units and oversight functions at MOFP. Weak PFM governance. Narrow dry season window (3-5 months) for ground movement of goods limiting accessibility. Significant delays in procurement processing. Nascent market with high costs for goods. Weak capacity of procurement staff in planning, process administration, contract management, and record keeping. Potential unavailability of local staff with required experience and import/work permit restrictions for foreign staff.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Hire experienced procurement consultant to support PMU and MOFP. Establish procurement committee at MOFP for contract oversight. Deploy MOFP procurement staff for capacity building. Use STEP system for all procurement activities. Conduct regular market assessments. Coordinate for timely processing of work permits for foreign staff. Prior and post-procurement reviews by World Bank. Use World Bank Standard Procurement Documents. Maintain adequate record keeping and storage. Publish procurement information and quarterly complaint action reports. Conduct independent procurement reviews as needed.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of potential resistance to changes brought about by PFM reforms. Political economy challenges and human resource capacity constraints. Difficulty in establishing critical mass of staff necessary for institutional capacity strengthening.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Formulate appropriate change management strategies. Seize opportunities in 'reform space' as they emerge. Sequence interventions with PFM foundations established first. Identify 'islands of reform' where changes are possible. Work with change agents at various government levels to drive reform agenda. Ground PFM reforms in R-ARCSS and underline link with citizens. Demonstrate success to hold officials accountable. Ongoing review of key PFM legislation expected to provide strong basis. Early gains expected to appeal to government responsiveness to change. Inclusion and active participation of civil society in PFM oversight.

identified_risks.risk_description: Climate change and natural disasters compound humanitarian situation and impede recovery. South Sudan ranked among 10 most affected countries globally (2019). Severe droughts (2011, 2015) and floods (2014, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021) causing casualties, displacements, livestock loss. May 2021 floods worst since 1962 affecting 9 of 10 states. Climate-related hazards intensify conflict over natural resources, driving displacement and food insecurity. Loss or damage to critical government information systems and budget/payroll systems disrupts business continuity and service delivery.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Cloud-based FMIS solution improves resilience against climate disasters by enabling off-site infrastructure maintenance and business continuity. E-waste management protocol to guide proper use and safe disposal of electronic equipment. Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared following consultations with MDAs and state representatives. Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) disclosed in-country and on World Bank website. Project activities communicated using appropriate language, media, and content at national, county, payam, and boma levels. Citizen feedback mechanisms incorporated in project activities.

identified_risks.risk_description: South Sudan remains fragile with history of large-scale civil war (2013, 2016) and ongoing subnational conflict. Violence rose significantly in 2020 relative to 2019. Continued economic progress hinges on accelerating implementation of R-ARCSS and reducing violence. Protracted conflict causes citizens to withdraw from government engagement. Loss of livelihoods and disempowerment demoralizes civil service.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project approach recognizes political context determines what is possible. Design draws on lessons from PFM reforms in other FCV countries. Project seeks to deepen capacities of key technical staff so core treasury functions continue despite potential conflict reemergence. Focus on creating windows of improved management of public finances. Identify and implement practical tools to mitigate uncertainty for priority spending areas. Improve discipline, transparency, and predictability in key spending areas. Support implementation improvements in focus sectors for sustained results. Agile and flexible approach to design and implementation with on-the-ground project management. Effective lesson learning so successes foster further achievement and corrective action addresses shortcomings.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1) - Relevant
Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (ESS10) - Relevant
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS4) - Relevant
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7) - Relevant
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanism established with complaint uptake, assessment and analysis, resolution and closure, grievance registry, and monitoring and evaluation. Designed in culturally appropriate way as outlined in Stakeholder Engagement Plan. Communities and individuals may also submit complaints to World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) or World Bank Inspection Panel. GRM will be accessible to public and include provisions on sexual exploitation and abuse and sexual harassment.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project addresses gender equality issues in MOFP where as of January 2022 there are no female Director Generals or Directors. Activities include: (1) Conducting organizational review and employee survey to obtain gender-disaggregated data on skills, job positions, and grade levels; (2) Adopting electronic salary payment system to collect gender-disaggregated data on civil servants; (3) Providing leadership, soft skills, and technical training targeting women; (4) Tracking participation of female civil servants in all trainings and capacity building activities through results indicator; (5) Targeting equal participation of men and women in capacity building activities for oversight institutions and community groups; (6) Introducing gender considerations in selected budget programs and collecting gender-disaggregated data; (7) Empowering female public servants through skills development for career progression.
vulnerable_groups: Displaced persons and refugees (due to conflict and climate disasters)
Rural communities dependent on subsistence farming and pastoralism (affected by extreme weather)
Low-income populations dependent on government services
Disadvantaged groups targeted for capacity building activities

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context. Approach recognizes uncertain political context determines what is possible. Design draws on lessons from PFM reforms in other FCV countries (Afghanistan, Cambodia, DRC, Kosovo, Liberia, Sierra Leone, Tajikistan, West Bank/Gaza). Project seeks to deepen capacities of key technical staff so core treasury functions continue despite potential conflict reemergence. Focus on creating windows of improved management of public finances and identifying practical tools to mitigate uncertainty for priority spending areas. Emphasis on improving discipline, transparency, and predictability in key spending areas. Project recognizes that reform gains can be reversed and political economy challenges exist outside project scope. Agile and flexible implementation approach with on-the-ground project management. Project supports transition from third-party to government implementation of World Bank projects to rebuild public trust and legitimacy of state. Building technical capability for transparency ('who gets what') coupled with early citizen involvement to address resource allocation questions at root of conflict.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan is world's youngest country (independence 2011) but remains highly fragile. Large-scale civil war in 2013 and 2016 reversed development gains. Country experiences widespread poverty, subnational conflict, weakened institutions, untapped human capital, poor service delivery, and non-diversified economy. Conflict and economic volatility have undercut development potential and exacerbated humanitarian situation. After contracting 5.4% in FY2020/21, economy projected to grow 1.2% in FY2021/22 but faces production bottlenecks in oil sector with dwindling production. Country at high risk of debt distress (59.5% of GDP). Macroeconomic risks remain high despite recent stabilization of exchange rate and inflation. Government has initiated reform program anchored by IMF Staff Monitored Program focusing on fiscal discipline, monetary/exchange rate reform, debt management, and governance strengthening. Revitalized Agreement on Resolution of Conflict (R-ARCSS, 2018) provides framework for peace and reform commitments. Fundamental PFM and procurement skills inadequate across government. Salary arrears (3-7 months) and low salary bases translate into low motivation, transparency, and accountability. Weak capacity compounded by collapse of disciplined environment due to continuous senior management changes.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security sector broadly accounts for more than 40% of total budget over past three fiscal years, leaving few resources for service delivery and human capital building. Public employment concentrated in security and rule of law functions (9 in 10 public sector employees). Challenges in payroll management pose significant fiscal risks. Integrity issues concerning public payroll particularly for organized forces. Violence rose significantly in 2020 relative to 2019. Continued economic progress hinges on accelerating implementation of R-ARCSS and reducing violence. Project supports timely payment of security sector salaries as critical for maintaining government legitimacy and reducing conflict drivers. Improved budget transparency and accountability expected to contribute to building trust between citizens and government, supporting durable peace.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - South Sudan experiences significant displacement due to conflict and climate disasters. Since independence in 2011, country suffered severe droughts (2011, 2015) and floods (2014, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021) resulting in high casualties, displacements, and livestock loss. May 2021 floods (worst since 1962) affected 9 of 10 states with estimated direct damages of US$671 million. Climate-related hazards intensify conflict over natural resources, driving population displacement and worsening food insecurity. Rural communities particularly affected by extreme weather events. With strong reliance on subsistence farming and pastoralism, livelihoods severely impacted. Project recognizes vulnerability to climate change and natural disasters compounds humanitarian situation and impedes recovery. Cloud-based FMIS solution designed to improve resilience and business continuity in event of natural or man-made disaster, protecting critical government information and budget/payroll systems from loss or damage.
